parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded internal tank. This is indicated by the rusty brown water visible in the drip tray and the leak from the pipe connection, which aligns with your description of rusty water and a leak.

This issue typically develops when the sacrificial anode inside the geyser tank has been fully consumed, allowing the tank's steel walls to begin rusting. Over time, this corrosion weakens the tank, leading to leaks and reduced thermal efficiency.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, leading to a larger leak or a complete burst of the geyser tank. This could cause significant water damage to your property, including ceilings, walls, and flooring.

There is a risk of electrical shorting if water comes into contact with the geyser's electrical components. It is important to switch off the geyser's power at the distribution board immediately.
image_descriptions: This image shows a black plastic drip tray beneath a geyser, containing a significant amount of rusty brown water. A copper pipe with a brass fitting is visible above the tray, with water actively dripping from a connection point into the tray.

This close-up image focuses on the copper piping and white flexible pipes connected to the geyser, revealing some visible corrosion and mineral deposits around the copper pipe entry point into the wall. The white pipes appear to be insulation or secondary lines.
This image provides an external view of a residential building with multiple windows and a brick wall, offering context of the property's exterior but no direct evidence of the geyser fault.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows a black plastic drip tray beneath a geyser, containing a significant amount of rusty brown water. A copper pipe with a brass fitting is visible above the tray, with water actively dripping from a connection point into the tray.

This close-up image focuses on the copper piping and white flexible pipes connected to the geyser, revealing some visible corrosion and mineral deposits around the copper pipe entry point into the wall. The white pipes appear to be insulation or secondary lines.
This image provides an external view of a residential building with multiple windows and a brick wall, offering context of the property's exterior but no direct evidence of the geyser fault.
contractor_checklist: Isolate the electrical supply to the geyser and shut off the main water valve.
Inspect the geyser for the source of the leak and confirm the extent of internal tank corrosion.
Drain the existing geyser and safely remove it from the premises.
Install a new geyser unit, ensuring all plumbing and electrical connections are correctly made and certified.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Switch off the geyser's circuit breaker at your main electrical distribution board and turn off the main water supply to your property to prevent further damage.
parsed.diy_verification: Check the colour of the hot water from your taps; if it is also rusty or discoloured, it further confirms internal tank corrosion.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated rusty water and increased electricity bill
Visible rusty brown water in drip tray
Symptoms align with corroded tank failure mode
parsed.cascading_damage: Water damage to surrounding area, increased electricity consumption

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing or broken spring on one side, causing the door to sag and preventing it from operating correctly. This aligns with your observation that the door opens partially, the motor beeps, and then it closes again.

Garage door springs, whether torsion or extension types, are crucial for counterbalancing the door's weight, allowing the motor to lift it with minimal effort. When a spring breaks or goes missing, the motor is forced to bear the full weight of the door, leading to strain, misalignment, and operational failure.

Continued attempts to operate the door with a missing or broken spring can cause significant damage to the garage door opener motor, the tracks, and other mechanical components. The uneven load can bend or warp parts not designed to handle the door's full weight, leading to more extensive and costly repairs.

Garage door springs are under extremely high tension and can be very dangerous if handled incorrectly. Attempting to repair or replace them without proper training and tools can lead to serious injury.
image_descriptions: This image shows the right side of the garage door, where the lifting arm and track are visible. The counterbalance spring assembly is notably absent from this side, leaving the door unsupported.
This wider view captures the entire garage door from inside, clearly illustrating its significant tilt and sag on the right side, consistent with a missing or failed counterbalance spring.
This close-up focuses on the central connection point of the garage door opener motor to the door itself. The red emergency release cord is visible, and the connecting arm appears intact.

This image provides a detailed view of the top bracket where the motor's connecting arm attaches to the garage door. The bracket and its fasteners appear secure, showing no immediate signs of damage at this specific point.

This image provides a detailed view of the top bracket where the motor's connecting arm attaches to the garage door. The bracket and its fasteners appear secure, showing no immediate signs of damage at this specific point.
contractor_checklist: Inspect the garage door system to identify the exact type and specifications of the missing or broken spring.
Replace the missing or broken spring with a new one matched to the door's weight and dimensions.
Rebalance the garage door to ensure smooth and even operation.
Adjust and lubricate all moving parts, including cables, rollers, and hinges, to prevent future issues.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Ensure the garage door is disconnected from the opener motor by pulling the emergency release cord, and avoid attempting to open or close the door manually.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged, if the door is extremely heavy to lift manually on one side, it confirms a missing or broken spring on that side.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'spring is missing on one side'
Image 1 clearly shows the absence of a spring on the right side
Image 2 shows the door is visibly tilted and sagging on the right side
Symptoms described (partial opening, motor beeping, closing again) are consistent with an unbalanced door
parsed.cascading_damage: Bent connecting rod or motor strain

parsed.message:
The rusty brown water pooling in your safety drip tray indicates that water is escaping from the geyser cylinder or its immediate fittings and reacting with the metal casing. When a geyser leaks and is accompanied by a drop in hot water duration and an increased electricity bill, it typically means the internal heating element has become heavily coated in limescale or has failed, and the inner cylinder itself may have corroded through.

This develops over time as the sacrificial anode rod inside the tank degrades. Once the anode is completely consumed, incoming water begins to corrode the steel tank and the heating element sheath, leading to rust, structural failure of the cylinder, and electrical inefficiency as the element struggles to heat the water.
parsed.action_required:

When a geyser leaks and is accompanied by a drop in hot water duration and an increased electricity bill, it typically means the internal heating element has become heavily coated in limescale or has failed, and the inner cylinder itself may have corroded through.

This develops over time as the sacrificial anode rod inside the tank degrades. Once the anode is completely consumed, incoming water begins to corrode the steel tank and the heating element sheath, leading to rust, structural failure of the cylinder, and electrical inefficiency as the element struggles to heat the water.

An under-sink or wall-cavity view showing copper and white plastic pipes passing through a rough wall opening above a wooden surface with visible dark water stains.
An outdoor view of an apartment building walkway showing the exterior wall where the geyser overflow pipes likely vent to the outside.

structured_clarification.intro: A few different issues can cause rusty water and poor heating performance. Answering this question will help us pinpoint the exact fault.

hypotheses.label: Corroded Geyser Inner Cylinder
hypotheses.why: Rusty water in the tray usually indicates the steel tank has corroded and breached.
hypotheses.discriminating_question: Is water actively dripping or spraying from the body of the cylinder itself?

hypotheses.label: Failed Geyser Heating Element
hypotheses.why: A rapid loss of hot water and high bills point to a failing or scaled-up heating element.
hypotheses.discriminating_question: Does the water get warm at all, or is it completely cold?

escape.prompt: Doesn't match what you are experiencing? Tell us more about where the water is coming from.

parsed.message:
The garage door is missing its left-hand tension spring, which acts as the counterbalance to offset the heavy weight of the wooden panel. When one spring is absent, the door becomes severely unbalanced and sags on that side during operation. This uneven load causes the motor to detect excessive resistance, triggering its safety overload sensor to stop and reverse the door to prevent motor damage.

These springs operate under extreme tension and fatigue over hundreds of cycles until they eventually snap or detach. Attempting to operate the door with only one spring places immense strain on the remaining spring, the lifting arms, and the motor drive gear, which can lead to further mechanical failure.
parsed.action_required:
The garage door is missing its left-hand tension spring, which acts as the counterbalance to offset the heavy weight of the wooden panel. When one spring is absent, the door becomes severely unbalanced and sags on that side during operation. This uneven load causes the motor to detect excessive resistance, triggering its safety overload sensor to stop and reverse the door to prevent motor damage.

These springs operate under extreme tension and fatigue over hundreds of cycles until they eventually snap or detach. Attempting to operate the door with only one spring places immense strain on the remaining spring, the lifting arms, and the motor drive gear, which can lead to further mechanical failure.

A wide view of the entire wooden tilt-up door showing a heavy vertical tension spring on the right side and a completely empty space where the matching spring should be on the left.
A view looking up at the central ceiling-mounted motor rail, the red emergency release cord, and the metal towing arm connected to the top of the door.
A close-up of the silver mounting bracket bolted to the top wooden frame of the door, securing the end of the motor's towing arm with a clevis pin and cotter pin.
